top_tweets.author: FutureVibesAi
top_tweets.text: Creative Your Burnout Morning Poster (Customized to Your Personality & Aesthetic) Created using @AdobeFirefly Partnered Model Prompt - Use my uploaded face as the ONLY facial reference. Preserve my exact identity, facial structure, expressive eyes, skin tone, lips, nose shape, and recognizable features with zero facial drift. Create an ultra-detailed cinematic 3D chibi-style portrait inspired by premium Pixar and modern collectible character art. The character should represent an exhausted but genius-level AI creator girl after an all-night creative session. Scene: She is sitting sleepily on the edge of a rustic wooden table early in the morning, looking emotionally drained but unintentionally adorable. Messy fluffy hair tied loosely in a chaotic bun with strands falling everywhere, sleepy half-open eyes, soft pout, slightly red nose, tired “I edited all night” expression. She wears oversized pastel sleepwear with subtle creative-tech aesthetics: soft ivory pajama set tiny “Future Vibes AI” embroidery cozy cardigan slipping off one shoulder fluffy duck slippers delicate pearl earrings Environment: Warm moody morning atmosphere with soft cinematic lighting, steam rising from a cup of coffee beside her, blurred artistic studio background, creative workspace energy. Around the character include subtle hints of her personality: tiny floating holographic video timelines miniature film clapperboard AI art sketches tiny glowing travel icons scattered sticky notes with creative ideas small laptop showing unfinished edits scribbled concepts YouTube play icon soft pastel doodles Expression & Mood: She should look like: an overthinking creative genius emotionally exhausted but lovable sleep-deprived filmmaker energy “just one more edit” vibe quiet chaos mixed with soft humor Style: Ultra-premium stylized realism, collectible figurine quality, ultra-detailed hair strands, realistic fabric texture, soft skin shading, cinematic depth of field, dreamy muted pastel tones, cozy Pinterest aesthetic, subtle humor, emotional storytelling. Lighting: Soft window light, warm cinematic shadows, gentle volumetric haze, realistic steam glow from coffee. Color Palette: Mocha brown, warm cream, dusty peach, muted teal, ivory, soft caramel, pastel gold accents. Composition: Centered symmetrical composition, shallow depth of field, highly expressive face, clean luxury aesthetic, social-media-ready, 8K ultra detailed. Add tiny handwritten doodles around the scene: “rendering…” “creative chaos” “future vibes” “1% battery” “one more export” Negative Prompt: bad anatomy, distorted face, extra fingers, plastic skin, overexposed highlights, blurry eyes, cartoonish proportions, low detail, bad hands, uncanny face, oversaturated colors, duplicate objects, warped furniture, messy composition, low resolution.

top_tweets.author: trynullsec
top_tweets.text: AI-generated software is becoming its own software supply chain. @cursor_ai, @Lovable, @Replit, @boltdotnew,@claudeai, @grok, agents, dApps, MCP tools, all of them are moving code from prompt to production faster than traditional security workflows can keep up. That creates a new requirement: security validation has to become model-native. Nullsec-S1 is being built for that layer. A security-specialized LLM that sits between AI-generated code and production, analyzing auth logic, secrets, env exposure, SQLi, SSRF, XSS, MCP/tool abuse, shell execution, dependency risk, wallet flows, and safe-code calibration. AI builds the software. Nullsec-S1 validates it.

top_tweets.text: .@character_ai has over 20 million monthly users — including kids and other vulnerable Pennsylvanians who trust the information they’re getting from these AI chatbots. top_tweets.author: obsidian_fr1
top_tweets.text: The Golden Standard of Snacks. GPT image 2.0 on @ChatGPTapp Prompt: Create an ultra-premium luxury advertising poster for a famous snack brand, designed by a world-class graphic designer, luxury branding expert, and award-winning advertising art director. Showcase the product as the hero at the center of the composition, larger than life, surrounded by dynamic product elements floating in dramatic motion. Incorporate cinematic lighting, realistic water droplets, condensation effects, glossy reflections, premium splash effects, floating particles, seasoning dust, and ultra-detailed textures that emphasize freshness, quality, and flavor. Use a sophisticated luxury color palette inspired by the brand, combining deep blacks with vibrant accent colors, metallic highlights, and rich contrast. Place a bold oversized logo at the top as the main visual anchor. Add a powerful tagline beneath it and display only 3 premium product specifications in elegant minimalist typography. The composition should be clean, balanced, and highly professional, with strong visual hierarchy and luxury branding aesthetics. Use dramatic rim lighting, volumetric light beams, depth of field, realistic shadows, premium studio photography techniques, and high-end commercial advertising styling. Create a sense of motion, energy, freshness, and exclusivity while maintaining a modern luxury feel. Behance-featured quality, Cannes Lions award-winning creative direction, Vogue-level art direction, billboard-ready layout, hyper-realistic rendering, photorealistic textures, masterpiece branding campaign, ultra-detailed 8K commercial poster, visually unforgettable, designed to look like a global advertising campaign from a top international creative agency.

top_tweets.author: akshay_pachaar
top_tweets.text: this is the most underrated update in the agent space right now. your AI workflow runs for 47 minutes, burns 312 LLM calls, then crashes at step 8. most frameworks make you restart from zero. @crewAIInc just shipped checkpointing. think google docs autosave, but for your agent's work-in-progress. every flow method becomes a recovery point. resume in one line. fork from any saved state into a new branch. edit past outputs and watch changes ripple downstream. visual TUI to inspect everything. your pipelines stop being fragile one-shot jobs. they become resumable, inspectable, branchable processes. zero extra infra. 100% open-source. get started with CrewAI here: https://t.co/bPGE0cGOIm (don't forget to star it ⭐️)

top_tweets.author: gracegongGG
top_tweets.text: What do the CEO of @Snowflake, the CTO of @vercel, the VP of @cursor_ai, and I have in common? We're all speaking at Snowflake Dev Day on June 4. Alongside: The founder of @FactoryAI. The Head of Developer Experience at @OpenAI. The founder of @crewAIInc. The GP of @BlitzVentures. The career ladder everyone grew up climbing doesn't really exist anymore. The job market is rough, and AI isn't just helping us work, it's starting to do the work. So we're doing something about it. My session is an open AMA: Grow Your Career in the Age of AI. Arnnon Geshuri, Snowflake's Chief People Officer, is leading our panel. Voices from venture capital, academia, and the economy, getting real about what's changing, what to learn, and where the opportunities are. Come with your real questions. This is the room I've been working toward for years. The event is FREE. Registration link in the first comment. Beyond excited to be speaking alongside icons like @RamaswmySridhar, Benoit Dageville, @DenisePersson, Arnnon Geshuri, @joaomdmoura, @romainhuet, @cramforce, @tidocarriero, @matanSF, @jowyang, and @scottbudman. See you June 4. 🚀 #SnowflakeDevDay #AI #VentureCapital #FutureOfWork #AgenticAI

top_tweets.author: mati
top_tweets.text: What a week in Warsaw. 2,500 leaders, builders, founders, researchers, investors and public leaders came together for the @ElevenLabs Summit in Grand Theatre. There were a lot of moments that felt very special. Poland is full of energy and optimism right now. Our researchers and builders are at the frontier of AI, and the economy keeps growing at a pace that is hard not to be excited about. It was a privilege to bring so much of the ecosystem together in Warsaw - to celebrate how far we've come, and to talk about what we can build next A few personal highlights: - Welcoming the President and Deputy Prime Minister, who shared their perspectives on innovation, security and Poland’s future - with a lot of energy and optimism - Hosting former President Lech Wałęsa and showing him our original machine - assembled by @dabkowski_piotr in his Warsaw apartment at the very beginning - Previewing our newest, most expressive models yet, and live demoing the future of customer journey when booking experiences (ending with iconic voice of Piotr Fronczewski)! - Speaking with @RBrzoska about reinventing parcel delivery in Europe, and Piotr Zak about positioning an iconic Polish company, Polsat Plus Group for the decades ahead - Announcing our partnership with @LOTPLAirlines to transform customer experience with voice agents - Introducing investors from across Europe and the US to Polish founders and builders - including all our board members - thank you @slpierre, @JenniferHli, @andrew__reed - for the first time! - Having my parents come to an ElevenLabs event for the first time Thank you to everyone who came from across Poland, Europe and further afield!

top_tweets.author: natalienkhalil
top_tweets.text: More than 7 million research papers were published in 2025. That number is about to explode. Think about all of the research agents and AI writing tools that were developed last year: @OpenAI’s Prism, @elicitorg, @FutureHouseSF, @k_dense_ai, etc. What happens as adoption of those tools inevitably increases? What happens to volume when the average researcher becomes fluent in @claudeai Code? Scientific infrastructure and peer review was designed for a different world than the one we are entering. Peer review is in crisis. (Data: DOI-indexed articles on @OpenAlex_org)

top_tweets.author: yyjhao
top_tweets.text: Your website is now a mobile app. No rebuild. No extra code. Today we're launching mobile app export on @floothq - take any website you've built and export it directly as a native mobile app. Same code, zero changes. For those new here: Floot is a vibe coding platform where non-technical founders and business owners can build complete, production-grade apps end-to-end. Real databases, real backend, real hosting — not just pretty prototypes. And now, real mobile apps too. This feature is currently in preview, and we want early adopters to try it out. Comment "floot" below and I'll DM you a 30% discount code + 5,000 bonus credits to get started.

top_tweets.author: yyjhao
top_tweets.text: Most websites aren’t failing because they’re bad. They’re failing because search engines can’t see them. Today we launched Improved SEO Support on @floothq. You can now enable server-side rendering, set your primary domain, configure search metadata, and generate robots.txt + sitemap.xml directly inside Floot. Competitors like Lovable, Base44, and Replit still don’t support server-side rendering. With Floot, we’re focused on making sure the apps you build can actually be found. Comment “floot” and I’ll DM you a 30% code so you can try it out :)

top_tweets.author: yyjhao
top_tweets.text: Sometimes big milestones go unnoticed. While browsing our analytics, I realized @floothq quietly crossed a meaningful one: 1,000,000+ visitors across websites built with Floot in the last 90 days. That number represents real people using real products created by our builders. One of our biggest goals with Floot has always been to make a platform where you can build something that actually works. Seeing these numbers come together feels like proof that we’re heading in the right direction. A huge thank you to every builder who’s trusted Floot to bring their ideas to life. This is starting to feel real in a new way, and it’s only up from here.

top_tweets.author: yyjhao

So we're opening up our launch offer to everyone: 30% off for new users with code FLOOT30F Use this when you upgrade to any paid plan. top_tweets.author: henryhengzj
top_tweets.text: We are joining @Workday! This is a huge step forward for our mission to make building powerful AI agents easy and accessible to everyone. @FlowiseAI started as a weekend project with a simple idea: make it easier for people to build with LLMs visually. I remembered the first line of code was written while I was on a train from Limerick to Dublin. What followed was far beyond anything I imagined: an incredible community, millions of downloads, and adoption across industries. To everyone who’s supported us along the way—thank you. You’ve helped shape what Flowise is today. I’m especially grateful to @ycombinator for believing in us, to @harjtaggar for his mentorship from day one, and to my cofounder, @ChungYauOng who’s been in the trenches with me every step of the way. We're incredibly excited to be working alongside Jerry, Peter, and the broader Workday team. We share a common vision: enabling more people to build meaningful, production-grade AI agents. Flowise isn't going anywhere - we're doubling down. With Workday’s backing, we’ll have the resources to move faster, go deeper, and continue building in the open. We're just getting started!

top_tweets.author: Rufus87078959
top_tweets.text: I want to say a big thank you to my longtime friend @javilopen, who built a fabulous AI tool called Magnific. The platform was later acquired by @freepik, and today, Freepik has rebranded into @magnific. In 2023, when Javilopen founded Magnific, I was one of his earliest users. Javilopen gifted me a lot of credits: I think 10,000 in value. I have since been using these credits, but when Freepik purchased Magnific, I started using Freepik more. I have not used the legacy Magnific platform in two years. Today, I decided to check the legacy platform and discovered my credits are intact. This is unheard of; there is no other company that would leave an unpaid subscription intact for two years and counting. I'm very grateful to Javilopen for building this awesome tool. If you aren't using Magnific, I highly recommend you check it out.

top_tweets.author: PQabelian
top_tweets.text: 🚨Quantum threat to #Bitcoin is becoming real! A new @Glassnode analysis estimates that nearly 10% of Bitcoin’s total supply is “structurally unsafe” in the event of a major quantum breakthrough, capable of breaking current elliptic curve cryptography. The exposed supply includes: * Early Pay-to-Public-Key (P2PK) wallets * Legacy multisig structures * Certain Taproot outputs At the same time, nearly 70% of BTC supply remains unexposed to direct quantum threats, highlighting that the ecosystem still has time to prepare, but not indefinitely. The industry conversation is evolving from theoretical risk toward practical migration paths: 🔹 Post-quantum cryptography 🔹 Quantum-safe wallet standards 🔹 Address hygiene improvements 🔹 New output formats like P2MR The broader takeaway: Quantum resilience is becoming a strategic infrastructure discussion for crypto, not just an academic one. Read more: https://t.co/RkNYIWyNWr #PQAbelian #ABEL #PostQuantum #QuantumComputing #Blockchain

top_tweets.author: FundersVC
top_tweets.text: Top AI and compute-native projects by market capitalization, cross-checked across @glassnode , @coingecko , and @CoinMarketCap ↓ @NEARProtocol (Near Protocol / Ethereum) @bittensor (Custom L1) @DfinityToday (Custom L1) @render (Solana) @AskVenice (Base) @virtualprotocol (Base / Ethereum) @GoKiteAI (Avalanche / Custom L1) @grass (Solana) @graphprotocol (Ethereum / Arbitrum) @akashnet (Cosmos) Current market data suggests that institutional liquidity in the AI and compute-native segment remains concentrated across these protocols, although exact rankings vary depending on the data provider and category methodology. What remains consistent across the major analytics platforms is a fragmented infrastructure landscape: capital is not loyal to any single ecosystem and continues to follow execution efficiency, liquidity depth, and compute availability. Rather than a single AI supercycle, the market increasingly looks like a competition between underlying networks for the base layer of the emerging machine economy. @HyperliquidX and HIP-3 should currently be viewed as an emerging infrastructure angle rather than a confirmed core leader of the AI sector, at least for now 😅

top_tweets.author: tonygentilcore
top_tweets.text: We've talked about @Glean's Enterprise Knowledge Graph since 2019. People kept asking to see it, so we built a demo. Ask Glean something like: "show me my manager's projects and the team behind them," and watch it traverse the graph in real time, mapping work and people across the org. Very cool!

top_tweets.author: jainarvind
top_tweets.text: The future of enterprise AI is giving enterprises the ability to match the right model to the right kind of work. That’s why I’m excited that @NVIDIA Nemotron 3 Ultra is coming soon to @Glean. Nemotron 3 Ultra is a strong option for everyday work. It delivers 91% of frontier-model completeness on key metrics, with the cost profile and flexibility of an open model. As AI moves from experimentation into everyday work, cost and model choice become architecture decisions, not just product decisions. This is the broader shift I’m seeing: enterprises want the freedom to use frontier models where they matter most, and open models where they can drive scale more efficiently. They don’t want to bet the future of their AI stack on a single model family. With Glean, customers can choose from 30+ open and proprietary models and route work to the best model for the job across Assistant and Agents. Thanks to the NVIDIA team for the collaboration.

top_tweets.author: jainarvind
top_tweets.text: Can your AI answer these questions? - Who owns this customer escalation? - What changed on this project after last week's review? - Why did we decide to push the launch? @glean's Enterprise Graph connects people, projects, docs, and customers so AI can reason across how work actually gets done, instead of starting cold every time. More effective, less expensive. For a long time, people have asked us if they could actually see the graph. Now you can.

top_tweets.author: lilyyang169
top_tweets.text: Last Friday, @smallest_AI hosted a private dinner for community and GTM leaders building in AI-native communications - with friends from @stripe, @meetgranola, @AnthropicAI, @PLAUDAI, @rootlyhq, and @SentientAGI. What stood out wasn't just the energy in the room. It was how this group of forward-thinking leaders truly showed up and opened up within such a short time frame. At every dinner we host, we curate the guest list with intention - not just who we want to meet, but who each person in the room would genuinely want to sit across from, and why it makes sense for everyone at the table to be there. In one evening, we got to know each other, exchanged notes, and explored upcoming collaborations. The closers shared what actually moves large deals forward. The conference veterans shared which ones are worth attending - and the secrets to maximizing ROI. The event hosts aligned on future collaborations. Since community and GTM are, at their core, collaborative, one of the greatest forms of leverage in this work is the network you build. And the most meaningful connections come from rooms like this - dinners structured around genuine relationships as the foundation for what comes next. Grateful for everyone who made this room what it was. 🙏

top_tweets.author: sourceforge
top_tweets.text: In this discussion, we talked with Founder & CEO of @HourOneAI, Oren Aharon. Hour One is an AI-driven video production platform that uses lifelike avatars to create personalized business messages. It offers a range of use cases, from thought leadership to training and marketing. The platform is easy to use, with a SaaS desktop app and templates available. Hour One prioritizes data security and employs encryption and regular security audits. The company serves a wide range of businesses, from CEOs using avatars for thought leadership to sales teams using avatars for customer interactions. In the near term, Hour One plans to introduce real-time interaction and cinematic use cases. In the long term, the company envisions avatars being used for communication and recreation on a large scale. Hour One dispels the myth that AI-generated videos lack authenticity, emphasizing that the tools enhance creativity and productivity for video producers.

"It's not about what's the best generic model, it's about what is the best model we can build for you, for your enterprise, for your use case." "We're gonna have a lot of branded models. top_tweets.author: OptibaseIO
top_tweets.text: We're live on @ProductHunt today. Over the past two years we've helped teams like @Kajabi, @riverside, @heyjasperai, @memberstack and @pheedloop run structured A/B tests. And across 3000+ teams, we kept seeing the same problems: -No hypothesis before running a test. -Tests stopped after a few days. -"Winning" results that wouldn't survive a basic stats check. Experimentation works, but only if the process behind it is sound. So we built the course we wish most teams started with. 6 modules covering: • What to test first for maximum impact. • How to calculate sample size and test duration. • How to interpret statistical significance correctly. • How to build a repeatable experimentation system. Completely free. Tool-agnostic. If you're responsible for conversion, growth, or experimentation, this might be very valuable knowledge: https://t.co/S5vcERDekt

It's LA and all votes are not created equal, but I would put my money against Nithya getting 40% of the remaining votes.

top_tweets.author: JBSDC
top_tweets.text: The new KPOW index from @Kalshi hasn’t gotten the attention it deserves. It’s a big deal. @Katiebiber and I wrote about how KPOW is a critical change in how information travels: a single index that let’s political info travel up from the people, not just down from elites like media institutions, consultants, and analysts. KPOW turns political power into a conversation. Markets aren’t magic, but in an era when everything is up for debate, they allow people to put their money behind their opinions. That, at least, can be one last, best, source of light in a storm.

top_tweets.author: AIandDesign
top_tweets.text: I totally did it. Turned the @Kling_ai pixel clock into a lo-fi TuneShine device. Shows album covers automatically based on Apple Music now playing or with Shazam so it will even work when you’re spinning vinyl, CD or tapes. In lovely lo-fi 16x16 pixels. Claude reverse-engineered the entire BLE protocol with which iOS can talk to this thing and implemented it for me in a library I’m now using for this. Absolutely insane stuff! This stuff will all be open source so anyone with a Divoom TimeBox EVO device can play! They’re 50 bucks on Amazon. Very cool.

top_tweets.author: meh_agarwal
top_tweets.text: I'm a big big fan of open source models in Creative AI. It allows non big-tech labs to shine and build goodwill. But I'm super confused by this new set of models by @ideogram_ai (already open source) and @krea_ai (soon open source) Why are these models purely text to image and don't natively support image to image? I feel with Flux Kontext, everyone took a step forward with models to understand that professional workflows need image to image models, yet this feels a step backwards. Second, would love for image models to start benchmarking early on https://t.co/CR7PmhNOIB. We have tie-ups with all the major video labs, but I think a top image model needs to shine in the best benchmarking suite run by @KoyalAI :)

𝐋𝐚𝐧𝐠𝐟𝐥𝐨𝐰 𝐢𝐬 𝐚𝐧 𝐨𝐩𝐞𝐧-𝐬𝐨𝐮𝐫𝐜𝐞 𝐯𝐢𝐬𝐮𝐚𝐥 𝐛𝐮𝐢𝐥𝐝𝐞𝐫 𝐰𝐡𝐞𝐫𝐞 𝐲𝐨𝐮 𝐝𝐫𝐚𝐠, 𝐝𝐫𝐨𝐩, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐧𝐞𝐜𝐭 𝐀𝐈 𝐜𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐨𝐧𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐬—turning days of coding into minutes of clicking. 𝐌𝐢𝐥𝐯𝐮𝐬 powers the vector search behind the scenes—storing embeddings, retrieving context, and giving your LLMs the memory they need. Here's what you can build in 20 minutes: ✓ Deploy vector DB with one Docker command ✓ Build RAG pipelines—zero code required ✓ Connect your own documents ✓ Test queries and get accurate responses ✓ Export as version-controlled JSON 👉 Start building now: https://t.co/zK3hlM9dz4 ——— 👉 Follow @milvusio, for everything related to unstructured data!

top_tweets.author: thisiskp_
top_tweets.text: 🚨 Announcement Alert 🚨 I’m stoked to be hunting @getlindy Assistant on Product Hunt today! The real bottleneck at work isn't the thinking, it’s the stuff between the thinking. Follow-ups, scheduling, inbox triage, and meeting prep. Lindy is a truly proactive AI assistant that watches your calendar and inbox, then acts on its own all from the convenience of iMessage. > It triages emails. > It preps you for meetings before you even open the invite. > It takes notes and writes follow-ups automatically. The people saving two hours a day aren't prompting better. They are delegating better. Would love your support and feedback on the PH page 👇 Link here: https://t.co/YGY2hu3iZP

top_tweets.author: DataChaz
top_tweets.text: Clawdbot vibes, but with real security guardrails! This is what many of us wanted @openclaw to be: a true “text-to-action” assistant in iMessage, but secure ;) @getlindy triages inbox, preps meetings, and drafts replies without exposing keys or ports. Huge launch, @Altimor 👏

top_tweets.author: swader
top_tweets.text: Super excited to announce I joined @getlindy to work on a truly useful agentic future. We're not catering to the same 10k power users of coding harnesses here - we're tackling the next frontier of utility and life de-cluttering. The team is rock solid. World-class talent working on improving life and business at scale. @Altimor is a no-BS guy who cuts through red tape like it's a finish line, and the mission is exactly what I care about. I'll be applying everything I learned in my own experiments on memory, dark factories, context optimization, automation, footprint minimization (to reduce supply chain risk), and so much more.

top_tweets.author: karrisaarinen
top_tweets.text: Normally I wouldn’t share something from a competitor, but this one seemed interesting. Since day one, @linear has been built for the most ambitious teams in Silicon Valley and beyond. That was always intentional, and we're very happy to serve those teams. Teams at OpenAI, Coinbase, Ramp, and frontier companies across fintech, healthcare, aerospace, and more use Linear to build and ship. Even teams building supersonic jets, like Boom Supersonic. We’re now seeing companies switch to Linear every week, and the pace is accelerating. We’ll keep doubling down helping Silicon Valley and other high performing teams on the planet build better software, faster even if our competition decides not to.

top_tweets.author: DouglasGan
top_tweets.text: One piece of feedback keeps coming up: “Why aren’t you using AI agents to automate earning strategies?” We’ve also heard from many FX-exposed investors asking for something simple enough that they can plug into @claudeai, @ManusAI, or @perplexity_ai and deploy with minimal effort. So, we’re building something even simpler than https://t.co/yzIITXgEDx Soon, users will be able to simply park liquidity and allow it to be utilized across PSPs, neobanks, on/off ramps, remittance providers, marketplaces, and more. Many assumed yields would be small. Turns out, 8–15% for safer currencies and up to 500%+ for exotics. Looks pretty attractive to me. My savings account gives me less than 0.5% annually. Banks ask you to jump through hoops too: credit your salary, set up recurring spend, spend a minimum amount every month, and more. With significantly more effort, maybe that 0.5% becomes 3-4% at best. Money should work for me, not the other way around.

top_tweets.text: Most people use AI to get answers. Power users use AI to improve how they think. It's the quality of the questions they ask. AI doesn't replace critical thinking. It amplifies it. top_tweets.author: iiiwokk
top_tweets.text: How do you build a brain? Everyone's talking about world models and unified enterprise data that agents and people can reason against but I haven't seen one live. So we built it at @NotionHQ, on Notion! We built a lot of AI agents on the People Team but quickly hit a wall. An agent is only as capable as the data it can reach and without people data, HR agents can't do much. But anyone in this space knows how difficult it is to make people data AI-ready. There's a reason why Workday isn't in Claude today. We've been thinking about this problem space for a while and here's how we're experimenting. Here's an onboarding demo. While seemingly simple, an onboarding agent in Notion is actually pulling from a single source of (governed & permissioned) data to personalize each new hires experience and sending the manager automated updates in Slack. We're using Human Intelligence's MCP here, pulling dummy data from Workday + Ashby. It's super cool bc unlocking people data means having a truly unified data model. We can pull business data. Work context. All this will power the company of the future, and I'm excited we get to push the boundaries 😊

top_tweets.author: saniaspeaks_
top_tweets.text: Created with GPT image 2 on @openart_ai Prompt: Transform the uploaded image into a retro Windows MS Paint outfit-builder interface aesthetic. Keep the full-body cutout of the subject centered on a clean white canvas, as if placed inside an old Windows XP / MS Paint workspace. Layout details: Left side: classic MS Paint toolbar with pixel-style icons and old UI elements. A vertical column of floating wardrobe/accessory slots (hair, top, bag, pants, shoes, etc.) shown as detachable outfit pieces. Bottom: a pastel color palette bar similar to old Paint color swatches. Window title bar at top with simple retro naming (e.g. “untitled - Paint”). Subject appears like a digital paper doll / dress-up character. top_tweets.author: SimplyAnnisa
top_tweets.text: Created with seedance 2.0 on @openart_ai One continuous take, no cuts. Single flowing follow-cam oner. Ultra-realistic cinematic style. Follow cam locked behind the skater as she drops into a steep San Francisco-style street, the city stretching downhill before her. Wind rushes past as she rapidly gains speed. The camera stays behind her, racing downhill. She ollies over a parked car, then grinds a long handrail down a steep staircase, sparks flying beneath the board. Cable-car tracks and colorful row houses blur past at high speed. The chase camera swoops lower as she launches off a cresting hill, soaring over an intersection with the bay visible in the distance. She lands smoothly and carves sharply through the street. The camera gradually pushes closer, still in a single uninterrupted shot, as she rides along a banked corner and accelerates toward the camera. The golden-hour sun creates warm lens flares and dramatic highlights behind her. Epic cinematic orchestral score with powerful percussion, realistic skateboard wheel sounds, rushing wind, subtle city ambience, and environmental audio. No spoken dialogue. SHOT 2 The white craft slips between two massive floating landplates as they slide apart. The dark craft follows through the narrowing gap. Camera tilts and rolls as entire islands rotate like gears around them. SHOT 3 They drop into a low corridor formed by stacked magnetic debris fields, where floating metal fragments orbit like storms. The camera stays tight, threading through spinning structures and drifting industrial ruins held together by invisible force fields. SHOT 4 Inside a transparent control sphere embedded in a floating island, a lone operator floats weightless, watching as the craft streak past outside. Reflections ripple across the curved glass as islands rotate around the capsule. SHOT 5 The craft ascend through a vertical magnetic lift zone where islands align into a spiral staircase in mid-air. top_tweets.author: Predicti0r
top_tweets.text: TRENDING: FIFA WORLD CUP 2026 NATION TO REACH ROUND OF 16 My best risk/reward NO shortlist: > Scotland NO for 78c > Sweden NO for 77c > Iran NO for 83c This market on @Polymarket is not the same as to qualify from the group. To reach the Round of 16, a team must: > get out of the group > reach the Round of 32 > win one knockout match That makes the new World Cup format extremely important: > 48 teams > 12 groups > top 2 from every group qualify > 8 best third place teams also qualify > then comes a new Round of 32 The market can easily overprice teams that look capable of qualifying from the group. But qualification is not enough. A team can finish 2nd or 3rd, reach the Round of 32, and still get a terrible matchup. So for NO on I'm looking for teams with: > difficult group path > low chance to win the group > dangerous Round of 32 bracket if they finish 2nd > weak or limited attacking ceiling > important injuries or squad problems > too much dependency on one game state > not enough quality to win a knockout match That is why I like Scotland NO, Sweden NO and Iran NO. 1) Scotland NO for 78c Group C: Brazil Morocco Scotland Haiti This is one of my favorite NO spots. Scotland can absolutely fight for qualification from the group. But they need to win a knockout match. And that is where the problem starts. Why: Scotland are probably fighting for 2nd or 3rd place. Brazil are clearly stronger. Morocco are also stronger in tournament profile, athleticism, technical quality and recent World Cup experience. So Scotland realistic routes are: > finish 2nd behind Brazil > finish 3rd and hope to qualify > very unlikely win the group Scenario A: Scotland finish 2nd in Group C This is the best realistic scenario. But it is still not great. C2 plays F1. It means Scotland could face the winner of Group F. Group F: Netherlands Japan Sweden Tunisia So if Scotland finish 2nd, they probably get Netherlands, Japan or Sweden in the Round of 32. That is a very difficult matchup. Netherlands have far more individual quality. Japan have better technical structure and tournament discipline. Sweden have more attacking power with Isak and Gyokeres. Winning that knockout match is a very different task. Scenario B: Scotland finish 3rd in Group C If Scotland beat Haiti and maybe get a draw against Morocco, they can be alive. But 3rd place path is much less controllable. They can qualify, but then the bracket can throw them into a strong group winner. For NO, this is exactly what we want. Scotland might survive the group but still get a terrible Round of 32 opponent. Scotland summary: Scotland can qualify from the group. But this market requires more. They need to win a Round of 32 match. Their realistic 2nd-place route likely sends them into F1. Their 3rd-place route is unpredictable. The Billy Gilmour injury is also important. That makes Scotland NO for 78c one of the cleanest risk/reward options. 2) Sweden NO for 77c Group F: Netherlands Japan Sweden Tunisia This is a bracket risk NO. Sweden are not a weak team. Actually, they have a very dangerous attacking profile. Alexander Isak, Viktor Gyokeres, Anthony Elanga Dejan Kulusevski would normally be a major creator, but his absence is a big problem Sweden route is very dangerous if they do not win Group F. Why: Group F is very uncomfortable. Netherlands are the favorite. Japan are disciplined, technical and very hard to beat. So Sweden have a real chance to finish 2nd or 3rd. But that is exactly where the bracket becomes ugly. Scenario A: Sweden win Group F Very unlikely This is the bad scenario for NO. F1 plays C2. That could be Morocco or Scotland. In that case, Sweden would be very live. If Sweden win the group, YES becomes much stronger. But I do not see Sweden winning Group F as the base case. They need to finish above Netherlands and Japan. That is possible, but not the most likely outcome. Scenario B: Sweden finish 2nd in Group F This is the key NO thesis. F2 plays C1. And C1 is probably Brazil. That is the problem. If Sweden finish 2nd, they may run directly into Brazil in the Round of 32. That completely changes the market. Sweden can beat many teams. But Brazil in a knockout match is a very different level of difficulty. Scenario C: Sweden finish 3rd This is also rather good for NO. A 3rd place team can qualify, but the Round of 32 opponent becomes less predictable and usually harder. Sweden would lose control of their route. Sweden summary: Sweden are dangerous. But they probably need to win Group F to make YES truly strong. If they finish 2nd, they may get Brazil immediately. If they finish 3rd, the bracket becomes random and difficult. They have great forwards, but the path is dangerous. That makes Sweden NO for 77c a strong bracket risk play. 3) Iran NO for 83c Group G: Belgium Egypt Iran New Zealand Iran NO is the safest profile of the three. Not the biggest upside. But very logical. They need to survive the group and then win a Round of 32 match. Why Iran NO makes sense: Belgium are clearly stronger. Egypt have Salah and Marmoush. Scenario A: Iran finish 2nd in Group G G2 plays D2. Group D: USA Turkiye Paraguay Australia That means Iran could face USA, Turkiye or Paraguay. None of these matchups are easy. USA at home would be very difficult. Turkiye have far more technical and attacking quality. So even if Iran finish 2nd, their Round of 32 path is not comfortable. Scenario B: Iran finish 3rd Iran can maybe qualify as one of the best third place teams. But then the bracket becomes much worse. A 3rd place path usually means facing a group winner. For a team with limited attacking upside, that is bad. Iran summary: Iran can be uncomfortable. They can fight for 2nd. They can maybe reach the Round of 32. But to reach the Round of 16, they need to win a knockout match. Their G2 path likely gives them USA, Turkiye or Paraguay. Their 3rd place path is unpredictable. Their attack is limited without Azmoun. That makes Iran NO for 83c a strong conservative NO. Final summary: The new World Cup format makes NO markets very interesting. Because many teams can qualify from the group. But not all of them are good bets to reach the Round of 16. The key is the Round of 32. My best NO shortlist: > Scotland NO for 78c > Sweden NO for 77c > Iran NO for 83c @PolymarketTrade @PolymarketSport

top_tweets.author: omarsar0
top_tweets.text: I highly recommend this. The Agentic Review is a new podcast from @QodoAI hosted by Itamar Friedman and Nnenna Ndukwe, and it's a great AI coding show that's neither hype nor doom. It's honest conversations about what shipping high-quality AI-generated code actually looks like. We need more of these conversations around AI agents. As an AI engineer, I think about this stuff constantly. A few things make this show worth your time: * It's a conversation about what good code means in the era of coding agents. * The hosts actually push back at guests instead of doing softball interviews. * The current guest lineup is strong: @dexhorthy, @shanselman, and @fullstackpython. I work a lot on context engineering, so the Dexter Horthy episode resonates the most for me. His take on context engineering as one of the biggest moats right now matches exactly what I'm seeing in production. He talks about a five-month experiment where his team stopped reading the code, then ripped it all out and rebuilt it by hand. That lesson about owning your context and actually reading what your agents produce is something every AI engineer needs to understand today. The bigger thesis across episodes: typing code may be dying, but the SDLC, code review, and craft matter more than ever. AI sprinkled on a broken software lifecycle is "a band-aid on cancer" (Hanselman's line, and it stuck with me). Thanks, @QodoAI, for the partnership on this post.

top_tweets.author: itamar_mar
top_tweets.text: This week, we shipped @QodoAI Findings. It's the natural next step from PR-by-PR review. Here's the problem it solves. If you manage an engineering team right now, you can probably answer a question about any single pull request your team shipped this week. What you likely cannot answer, at least not quickly, is the version of that question that spans your whole org. > How many issues were caught and fixed across our repos this month? > Which services are accumulating the most risk? > Is review quality on the payments team improving or sliding? > Are the same issues getting flagged for the same reasons over and over? These are the questions that come up in staff meetings, even in board prep these days, in conversations with security... Answering them used to mean stitching together Slack threads, asking around, or pulling a spreadsheet by hand. Qodo Findings, consolidates every issue Qodo has surfaced across your reviewed pull requests into one view. Three parts that work together. A top row of analytics: total critical findings, percentage resolved, average findings per PR. Volume, throughput, trend. The high-level read without having to open anything. Filters underneath: by repo, by owner, by issue type. Cut the data the way you actually need it. Pull up only security findings. Look at a single team. Focus on one service. A full searchable, sortable list of every issue, with the ability to drill straight into the underlying PR when something stands out. Available now in beta on GitHub, GitLab, BitBucket, and Azure DevOps. Would love to get any feedback even DM me! Taking a step back. The point isn't a new dashboard. The point is that the review layer has to operate at a different altitude now. PR-by-PR is necessary, but it isn't sufficient. When code is being generated continuously across teams and repos, leaders need to see across teams and repos. The same way they see across any other part of their operation. This is the first release in that direction. More coming. We care about the software health as a whole, not only PR-by-PR, and there is a lot of innovation needed here. top_tweets.author: ApexImperialist
top_tweets.text: I started using @TheQuillBot instead of Grammarly since the latter degraded the quality of their free version. QuillBot allows me to tether my writings to specific versions of English (UK, US, AU, etc.), & what I've noticed is that my English is a mixture of UK & US, confusing the AI's checker ... I'm not sure how interesting that is to others, but I find it kind of interesting & I assume it is the result of having learnt British English from my dad & from school, while having interacted with Americans for a long time. But this might signify that the various versions of English might mix up due to the internet, leading to a more spectrum-defined version of it that accepts different types of spelling in the future.

top_tweets.author: timsemelin
top_tweets.text: 🔥@ReadyPlayerMe has been acquired by Netflix. The company started over a decade ago as a small avatar experiment by young builders from Estonia. What began as a niche idea around digital identity has grown into a widely used avatar platform across games and virtual worlds. As part of the acquisition, the Ready Player Me team will join Netflix’s gaming division to work on cross game avatar technology. The focus is on enabling players to carry their identity and fandom seamlessly across different games and experiences. The journey involved years of experimentation, failed ideas, rapid learning, and close collaboration with thousands of developers. That process ultimately shaped Ready Player Me into a scalable product with global adoption. The acquisition aligns with a long held vision of open avatars that are not locked into a single game or platform. With Netflix’s scale and reach, that vision now has the opportunity to reach a much broader audience. This move marks another step in Netflix’s expansion into gaming and interactive experiences.

top_tweets.author: wangyuanzju
top_tweets.text: Quick comments on ChatGPT Atlas Today, OpenAI launched its own AI browser, ChatGPT Atlas, which highlights its abundant resources and restless energy. However, ChatGPT Atlas shows little innovation — it’s roughly equivalent to Dia plus @remio_ai 1.0. We introduced browser memories in remio seven months ago, and we believe that this data belongs to the user. Therefore, we store all of it locally on the user’s computer, rather than doing what OpenAI does — keeping the data on their cloud servers and not allowing users to export it. Data belongs to the user and should be freely processed with whichever large model is most suitable. Users have this right — any attempt to forcibly bind user data to a specific model can only stem from capital interests, not user interests.

top_tweets.author: HenrM_Cruz
top_tweets.text: A few thoughts after seeing this announcement blow up and building the @RowsHQ AI Analyst for the past 3 years: 1. It reinforces the idea that spreadsheets aren't going anywhere. It's the only common product where any businessperson can look at and analyze data. You saw this with Claude, the Ramp labs release, Shortcut, and many others. Frontier labs are realizing this as well. 2. LLMs will go after the application layer. You can use a chat interface to ask questions, write copy, or draft an email, but the end result happens in a productivity tool somewhere else. This "somewhere else" is where collaboration happens, where (very) high margins live and where you don't need to worry about inference costs or hallucinations. 3. Spreadsheets are almost code, that's why this works. 4. Data analysis agents are world-class when they can handoff spreadsheet formulas to Python when needed. We learned that last year when we added support for Python in @RowsHQ AI Analyst. The agent automatically decides if it can solve the request using spreadsheet formulas (easier for 1B people to understand), or if it needs Python. 5. For this to ultimately replace most spreadsheet use, it needs a System 1 - System 2 type system, so that easy requests are handled very fast (routed to simpler models with less context), and hard problems take as much time as they need. That's how we architected the AI Analyst, which results in System 1 tasks taking a median of 6s (e.g. add a column with profit) and System 2 tasks taking 69s. It won't matter how smart it is at creating a DCF if it takes 30 seconds to format a range of cells. Most knowledge work is editing existing work, not creating new one. 6. The next step-function improvement is having your data analyst agent accessible everywhere you work. This means opening and analyzing CSVs from your email inbox, reading pdf receipts, answering questions on Slack, etc. 7. If you really want to see what a best-in-class spreadsheet agent can do, try https://t.co/lndfMNCbZC.

top_tweets.author: HenrM_Cruz
top_tweets.text: This is how I use the AI Analyst in @RowsHQ to go over the website traffic to my book review blog and summarize it for me. "Do an analysis of my GA4 website traffic from October: website visitors, top source mediums, most visited pages. Compare with last October, and add a few charts and a text summary." This is a glimpse of how business teams will interact with their data: use AI to fetch your business data, analyze it, surface the results and allow you to audit the results and create a beautiful document that you can share with your team.

top_tweets.author: HenrM_Cruz
top_tweets.text: Hearing from @nearestnabors on local models at @lisbonai_ We’re currently working with a large customer on deploying @RowsHQ AI Analyst on top of local models and the trade-offs are clear: - Local: cheaper, works offline, great for task specific jobs. - Cloud: current models are just much smarter and can do a lot more. It does seem clear that as you project fwd the capabilities of SMLs, in 1-2 years we’ll start seeing large AI applications on top of local models.

top_tweets.author: ainativedev
top_tweets.text: .@Sourcegraph analysed 1,281 coding agent runs across large enterprise codebases and found the bottleneck often wasn’t the model — it was context retrieval and navigation. Agents struggled once codebases grew large enough: pulling in the wrong files, thrashing between tools, and making locally correct changes that broke systems elsewhere. One stat says a lot: a task that took one agent 96 tool calls and 84 minutes dropped to five calls and under five minutes with better retrieval infrastructure. The takeaway is this: coding agents need far better context and navigation layers around them to operate reliably in production. 🔗 Read the full story here: https://t.co/LsatesrRDC

top_tweets.author: AppenResearch
top_tweets.text: @AppenResearch independently evaluated @subquadratic's SSA kernel - a learned sparse attention mechanism designed to reduce the quadratic scaling limitations of full attention. Results at 1M-token context lengths: - 56.2× wall clock speedup vs. FA2 - 62.8× FLOP reduction (validated via torch.profiler, <4% variance from theoretical) - 95.6% average score across RULER tasks at 128K - 86.2% average score on the hardest MRCR 8-needle bucket (512K–1M contexts) - 81.8% SWE-Bench Verified resolved rate Full report: https://t.co/9OpvaqDPbt

top_tweets.author: layerlens_ai
top_tweets.text: LayerLens is partnering with @subquadratic to evaluate SubQ on Stratix. 🔍 Same benchmark suite every model goes through: reasoning, code generation, instruction following, tool use, and long-context 📊 Results published in Stratix Public alongside 200+ models including GPT 5.5 and Opus 4.7 🧬 First model on Stratix built on a non-transformer attention architecture 🔄 Stratix Enterprise for continuous evaluation across future SubQ releases SubQ uses Subquadratic Sparse Attention (SSA), an alternative to traditional transformer attention. SSA claims to massively increase context processing without sacrificing performance. A massive claim that has sparked a ton of debate. Transparency, openness, and auditability. That is the point. https://t.co/Z3mya0YK4y

top_tweets.author: YashasEdu
top_tweets.text: The cost to run GPT-3.5 level intelligence fell 280x in two years. On PhD-level reasoning tasks, inference costs are dropping 40x per year and now @subquadratic’ 52x cheaper long-context on top of that. Every major platform shift followed a compute cost collapse. + Cheap bandwidth gave us streaming + Cheap mobile compute gave us Instagram AI inference is collapsing faster than either of those curves. The apps that win next are the ones that use the most intelligence per dollar, not the least.

top_tweets.author: alexvoica
top_tweets.text: Today, I walked past the Old Street roundabout on my way to a SXSW London side event, and saw @synthesiaIO on a billboard with some of the best companies Europe has ever produced. I'll admit it gave me a much-needed energy boost ahead of speaking on yet another panel about AI's problems. The billboard is part of #BuiltinEurope, a campaign @balderton has launched to tell the truth about what is happening right now in European tech. The story we've heard for years has been one of catching up. Europe could lead, the headlines said, if only it had fixed this or that. That's because building in Europe was treated as a thing you had to overcome. Now, for the first time in a long while, the opposite is true. Met with indifference at best and opposition at worst at home, European founders are forced to build companies that work everywhere from the first day, and they're doing it with style (and substance). At Synthesia we feel this in our bones. We were built in London and we have been global from the start, because being European made that the only sensible way to think. So if you have ever thought about building a company in Europe, or joining one, consider this your invitation. https://t.co/cvFiwU9dG3 is now live, with a jobs platform pulling together open roles from a thousand European startups, and a directory of the continent's best incubators for anyone ready to start something of their own. Europe is building. Come build with us.

top_tweets.author: SebJohnsonUK
top_tweets.text: "AI companies have terrible retention" @synthesiaIO has an NRR of 140%. B2B AI has some of the highest retentions ever seen.

top_tweets.author: NinaDSchick
top_tweets.text: More great news from @synthesiaIO - one of the most innovative AI companies!

top_tweets.author: alexvoica
top_tweets.text: To be clear, @synthesiaIO is proud to be part of Built in Europe and the case @balderton makes is the right one. I am less comfortable when that ambition gets folded or confused with the version of sovereignty taking shape in Brussels, where the instinct is to reduce reliance on foreign technology through procurement rules, subsidies, and a thickening layer of complex and overlapping regulation. Real sovereignty comes from building generational companies strong enough to win on open ground. Shielding them from competition only swaps one dependence for another and creates inferior products and services at higher costs. We need more of the former, and less of the latter.

top_tweets.author: alexvoica
top_tweets.text: Today, @synthesiaIO's security team is announcing a new multi-agent system that allows us to keep pace with the high volume of AI-generated code shipping through our codebase: https://t.co/8prHK0qa9D As more companies use AI to write their software, they will inadvertently create security flaws which can then be exploited by bad faith hackers equipped with powerful AI models. Claude Mythos is expected to help with this issue, but the model is currently available only to select Anthropic partners, not the general public. Therefore, we are sharing the recipe for our tool so we can help any company approach Mythos-levels of security performance today, with off-the-shelf models that are generally available. Additionally, our recipe achieves this performance at a reduced cost, meaning it can be used by larger and smaller companies alike. @mtschammer, head of security at Synthesia, spoke with @natrubio__ from @theDeepView about the design decisions we've made and how this project came to life: https://t.co/TvhbwiYbqW

top_tweets.author: amuldotexe
top_tweets.text: SOP driven problem solving for using LLM tools like @windsurf_ai @cursor_ai @tabnine - based on 350+ hours of playing with them over the last few months - Create a new folder for context retention docs called aa_ref_01 - Create file named r01_initial_ideation.txt for just noting down all the ideas without any structure - Create file named r02_domain_analysis.txt which will contain the best of the conversation we have with tab nine or @web in order summarize the best domain knowledge needed for the problem statement - Create file named r03_prd_v1.txt which will be the first PRD for the user journey at a high level - Create file named r04_prd_v2.txt which hopefully is a more precise version of the previous PRD based on multiple iterations on the previous file - Create file named r05_arch_L1.txt which will contain the first attempt at the architecture of the PRD - this file should leverage a relationship of consistency with r04_prd_v2.txt + r02_domain_analysis.txt PAUSE HERE & NOTICE: a. LLMs should be treated as search + assimilation tools, you have to bias them with right keywords b. LLMs need to iterate to arrive at a more refined output c. LLMs need to sumarize information which will lost by the sliding context window, hence you constantly create new docs which are in sync with previous docs now - Create file named r06_arch_L2.txt which will contain the second iteration of the architecture of the PRD - this file should leverage a relationship of consistency with r05_arch_L1.txt + r04_prd_v2.txt + r02_domain_analysis.txt - Create file named r07_explain_architecture.txt which will contain the explanation of the architecture of the PRD - this file should leverage a relationship of consistency with r06_arch_L2.txt + r04_prd_v2.txt + r02_domain_analysis.txt. This file is basically a rubber-duck debugging of the architecture with us as the listener who wants to make sure that the r06_arch_L2.txt is well thought out - Create a file named r08_TDD_v1.txt which will basically ask the LLM to write a TDD plan to implement the r06_arch_L2.txt in the most minimalistic way possible, it should be consistent with r04_prd_v2.txt & r02_domain_analysis.txt - If the r08_TDD_v1.txt is good enough, then select each step of the TDD plan as a checkbox - and ask the strong LLM (code LLM) to implement the TDD plan in the most idiomatic bug-free way possible line by line, thoroughly following the TDD methodology of starting with Red Test, then Green Test, then Refactor - Create a file named r09_bug_log.txt to note copy all the bugs encountered in the process of implementing the TDD plan - Create a file named r10_avoid_bugs.txt which will contain meta patterns and best practices discovered during the implementation of the TDD plan - Create UAT plan for the whole feature, ideally this should be very close to r04_prd_v2.txt - and manually check the results, and document them in r11_uat_results.txt - Create a file named r99_unclassified.txt to note down all the prompts, ideas, mistakes, lack of knowledge or references or links which you encounter during the journey with timestamps PAUSE HERE & NOTICE: d. LLMs need to be asked to reason themselves so they can find their own logical fallacies, like rubber duck debugging e. LLMs need to be given reference files for patterns to avoid, be it via past bugs or anti patterns to avoid f. LLMs need to follow TDD because it makes it easier to arrive at the final code - it is an easier prediction problem if you know what will be the requisite final pattern, also helps you avoid mistakes early enough g. LLM whispering is a cumulative skill so constantly document your prompts, steps & learnings p.s. most won't be able to follow the above steps and would rather smash "fix this" every 2 minutes because they lack the patience to follow these steps methodically

top_tweets.author: amuldotexe
top_tweets.text: It's half past midnight since 2 pm I've been trying to get a postgres x go x react app to run on docker in a way such that it can deployed on railway @grok @tabnine @cursor_ai helped I did get the docker to run on my system but not on railway so the day ended in a mild disappointment I spent the first 2 hours doing something just with Cloudflare DNS manager trying to configure cmake for Google sites - and failed at it miserably I think I didn't zoom out for almost 8+ hours not a good sign - should've taken a break should've asked for help also, but somehow my ego won't let me ask for help from friends why can't I figure it out myself - the ego asks but it's a far better day than what I had expected it to be - at least I put in a few hours of focus - yes heavily cortisol driven, but still I gained a lot of familiarity there's a headache, the brain feels numb, but there's a sated joy - because you remember - you are still the teenager who'd obsess on a problem and lose a day on it of all things I learnt in Kota 20 years ago, this was the most useful skill: the ability to sit on a problem alone this lonely work is the difficult part - the LLMs help, but they don't quite solve everything, you still gotta keep talking to them, nudging them should've taken more breaks, but this is far better than not putting in the hours in the struggle maybe the adhd meds have started showing results on the focus side also or maybe it's too late & I can't think clearly either ways there's only one insight that our only job is to slow down time slow down time that's all we can do slow down time iykyk p.s. evening tea was good today

top_tweets.author: degensing
top_tweets.text: VoiceMoat vs Typefully A beautiful editor won't fix a generic voice. @Typefully is the best thread composer in the AI Twitter category. Not "good for an AI tool." Good by any standard. Keyboard shortcuts, thread blocks, split view, multi-platform publishing. @VoiceMoat is a voice model trained on your actual posts. Auden learns your 9 voice dimensions, generates drafts in your specific style, scores every output 0-100 against your profile. Both tools live in the X creator stack. Both have serious users. Both produce publishable content. Which is why the comparison keeps coming up. They solve different bottlenecks. A creator with Typefully but no voice model has a beautiful editor producing generic-AI drafts. The writing surface is great. The output sounds like everyone else using AI on X. A creator with VoiceMoat but no thread composer has voice-accurate AI in a functional interface. The output sounds like them. The composing experience is fine but not elegant. The right answer for most serious creators is both. When budget only allows one, the question is which problem is louder right now: If the writing surface itself is slow and fragmented.. or you publish to 5 platforms simultaneously.. or your voice is already strong and AI tools already sound like you.. Typefully. If AI drafts feel generic and your audience is giving you "did you use AI?" signals.. or you live in replies and need in-feed drafting.. or you want to measure voice drift over time.. VoiceMoat. The editor question and the voice question are separate. Know which one you're actually hitting.

top_tweets.author: Eli5defi
top_tweets.text: Distribution is king, and that’s never been more true for content creators. If you’re a creator using @typefully and @claudeai, you’re leaving a lot on the table if you’re not using this 3-step method to optimize your posts: - Set up your Typefully MPC in Claude - Ask Claude to run an analytical review of your post using the prompt below - Review and take action as per suggestion --- You are an expert Social Media Strategist and Data Analyst. Objective: Perform a comprehensive performance audit of my [Platform Name, e.g., X, LinkedIn, Threads] account using my exported analytics data from Typefully MCP. Provide a strategy that balances growth with time-efficiency. → Content DNA & ROI The “Stickiness” Factor: Identify which content categories (e.g., Educational, Personal Stories, News Updates, etc.) have the highest Save/Bookmark-to-Reach ratio. Which posts are people keeping for later vs. just scrolling past? The “Show More” Trigger: For long-form text or threads, analyze the hook (the first 2 lines). Which opening styles consistently get users to click and read the full post? Media vs. Message: Compare the performance of text-only posts vs. posts with images/video. Is the extra effort of creating visuals resulting in a proportional increase in Profile Visits or Link Clicks? → Time & Velocity Mapping The High-Engagement Window: Pinpoint the 60-minute windows when my audience is most “reactive.” Don’t just give me a time—explain the Engagement Velocity (how fast interactions happen in the first 30 minutes) and how it affects overall reach. The “Quiet Hour” Opportunity: Are there times when engagement is lower but reach is high? Identify whether I should post during off-peak hours to face less competition in the feed. → Repost/Retweet Spacing Strategy The Frequency Audit: I currently repost my own content at [Insert your intervals, e.g., 1hr - 6hr - 12hr]. Based on the data, is this causing “feed fatigue” (the same people seeing the post too many times), or is it successfully reaching new time zones? The 24-Hour Flywheel: Design an optimized repost schedule that covers three distinct global regions (e.g., morning in the US, EU, and Asia) without overlapping on a single user’s timeline more than once every 8 hours. → Conversion & Growth Funnel Follower Attribution: Which specific posts actually drove New Follows? Distinguish between "Viral Hits" (high views/low follows) and "Community Builders" (low views/high follows). Bio & Call-to-Action (CTA) Check: Based on the spikes in profile visits, suggest 3 ways to optimize my Bio or Pinned Post to convert more visitors into long-term followers. ---

top_tweets.author: BoysClubWorld
top_tweets.text: A pattern we've noticed on Show Me Your Stack: The best builders have a system for catching ideas before they disappear. @vercel founder, @rauchg showed @julesrosenberg Timeless, an internal tool he uses to capture bugs, product feedback, screenshots, screen recordings, and quick thoughts while he's in the middle of working. A few use cases he shared: > recording bugs the moment they happen > capturing UI feedback without writing a long ticket > saving product ideas before they disappear > sharing context with teammates asynchronously > creating a running backlog from real-world usage The common thread: less time documenting, more time noticing. What's interesting is how much of product development comes down to collecting observations before they're forgotten.

top_tweets.author: evilmartians
top_tweets.text: 80% of databases on @neondatabase are created by agents. 30% of new @supabase signups come from AI builder platforms, like Bolt. 10% of @vercel signups come via ChatGPT. This is likely something you're seeing as well. And if your tool isn't ready to offer a good agent experience, agents will use a competitor that is. But what does an AI agent need to have a good experience? The answer is programmatic access from signup to production. We build that layer. For those who follow us but don't know what we do, this is one of those things. We also add agent-aware security guardrails and put AI harnesses in place for better code production. We've already done it for @any_cable and can do it for your devtool too. See what we can do: https://t.co/Rnhjk32FQl

top_tweets.author: ycombinator
top_tweets.text: .@Veriff is the identity layer for the internet: matching people to government-issued IDs with a selfie so companies can verify users instantly and stop fraud at global scale. What began in Estonia has grown into one of the world’s leading identity verification systems, processing hundreds of millions of checks and analyzing thousands of signals per user. Founder @kaarelkotkas joined YC’s @gustaf to share how he went from a high-school prototype to serving global fintechs and marketplaces, why identity is an infrastructure problem, and why he sees a future where your digital passport might be issued not by governments — but by Veriff. 00:00 – How Veriff Started in a High School Computer Lab 01:52 – Why Online Identity Is an Infrastructure Problem 04:58 – Matching IDs With a Selfie 07:10 – Fighting Fraud in Fintech & Marketplaces 10:53 – Scaling Verification to a Global Platform 15:44 – YC, Early Breakthroughs, and Hard Lessons 20:31 – Deepfakes, Signals, and the New Threat Landscape 26:33 – Building a Mission-Driven Team 32:48 – The Digital Passport Vision

top_tweets.author: TheHackersNews
top_tweets.text: 🔒 Fragmented identity pipelines are failing against digital injection attacks. Hubert Behaghel, CTO at @Veriff, explains that stitching together multiple vendors for camera capture, liveness detection, and risk scoring often drops critical signals like device telemetry and session context. This creates easy entry points for attackers using virtual cameras and emulators. Integrated systems that maintain end-to-end visibility offer much stronger protection. Read: https://t.co/Ps7W15nqKb

top_tweets.text: Fading Through Beautiful Versions of Her Created with Seedance 2.0 on @ViduAI_official Prompt Title: Mirror Portal Dressing Room A hyper-stylish cinematic fashion scene inside a dim, futuristic dressing room filled with glowing mirrors and soft neon reflections. A single elegant female model stands in front of a tall mirror framed with subtle luminous edges. The atmosphere is quiet, dreamy, and surreal—floating dust particles in light beams, soft ambient hum. 0–3s: She slowly approaches the mirror. Her reflection is slightly delayed, almost alive. 3–6s: She gently touches the mirror surface. Ripples of light spread like liquid glass. 6–10s (Transformation Sequence): Each touch triggers a rapid aesthetic shift: • Vintage 70s outfit with warm film grain tones • Y2K glossy streetwear with metallic textures • Futuristic chrome bodysuit with holographic glow • Royal couture gown with gold embroidery and floating fabric physics The mirror acts like a portal—each outfit briefly “pulls through” from the reflection into reality. 10–13s: The camera orbits around her as the transformations sync with her movement. Lighting constantly reshapes the scene. 13–15s (Final Moment): She smiles slightly, resting her hand on the mirror. The surface stabilizes, showing all four versions of her reflected at once in split ethereal layers. Fade out with soft glow. Style keywords (add to generator): cinematic fashion film, surreal mirror portal, luxury aesthetic, soft neon lighting, high-detail fabric simulation, shallow depth of field, 4K, ultra-real, dreamy atmosphere, slow motion transformation. #vidu

top_tweets.author: smallest_AI

top_tweets.author: HenryBelcaster
top_tweets.text: SaaS marketing doesn't have to be boring (or just product explainers) Retention on this ad I made with @zapier was 82% Usually most ads in this space sit around 45-50% No bueno... As we work with more SaaS companies on sales enablement and explainer content, we've found that leading with a fun story almost always performs better. Specifically, I like leading with the use case, not the product. Show the pain point, the workflow, or the desired outcome first. Then introduce the software as the solution. @wadefoster @bryanhelmig
